author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

title: Preface to the Treatise on Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Augustin has made no mention of this treatise in his book of Retractations; for the reason, no doubt, that it belonged to the collection of the Epistles, for which he designed a separate statement of Retractations.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: In all the mss. this work begins with his usual epistolary salutation: “Augustin, to his holy brethren and fellow-bishops Eutropius and Paulus.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And yet, by general consent, this epistle has been received as a treatise, not only in those volumes of his works which contain this work, but also in the writings of those ancient authors who quote it.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Amongst these, the most renowned and acquainted with Augustin’s writings, Possidius (In indiculo, 4) and Fulgentius (Ad Monimum, i.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 3) expressly call this work “A Treatise on the Perfection of Man’s Righteousness.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: So far nearly all the mss. agree, but a few (including the Codd.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Audöenensis and Pratellensis) add these words to the general title: “In opposition to those who assert that it is possible for a man to become righteous by his own sole strength.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: In a ms. belonging to the Church of Rheims there occurs this inscription: “A Treatise on what are called the definitions of Cœlestius.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Prosper, in his work against the Collator, ch.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 43, advises his reader to read, besides some other of Augustin’s “books,” that which he wrote “to the priests Paulus and Eutropius in opposition to the questions of Pelagius and Cœlestius.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: From this passage of Prosper, however, in which he mentions, but with no regard to accurate order, some of the short treatises of Augustin against the Pelagians, nobody could rightly show that this work On the Perfection of Man’s Righteousness was later in time than his work On Marriage and Concupiscence, or than the six books against Julianus, which are mentioned previously in the same passage by Prosper.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For, indeed, at the conclusion of the present treatise, Augustin hesitates as yet to censure those persons who affirmed that men are living or have lived in this life righteously without any sin at all: their opinion Augustin, in the passage referred to (just as in his treatises On Nature and Grace, n.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 3, and On the Spirit and the Letter, nn.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 49, 70), does not yet think it necessary stoutly to resist.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Nothing had as yet, therefore, been determined on this point; nor were there yet enacted, in opposition to this opinion, the three well-known canons (6–8) of the Council of Carthage, which was held in the year 418.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Afterwards, however, on the authority of these canons, he cautions people against the opinion as a pernicious error, as one may see from many passages in his books Against the two Epistles of the Pelagians, especially Book iv. ch. x.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: (27), where he says: “Let us now consider that third point of theirs, which each individual member of Christ as well as His entire body regards with horror, where they contend that there are in this life, or have been, righteous persons without any sin whatever.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Certainly, in the year 414, in an epistle (157) to Hilary, when answering the questions which were then being agitated in Sicily, he expresses himself in the same tone, and almost in the same language, on sinlessness, as that which he employs at the end of this present treatise.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “But those persons,” says he (in ch. ii. n.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 4 of that epistle), “however much one may tolerate them when they affirm that there either are, or have been, men besides the one Saint of saints who have been wholly free from sin; yet when they allege that man’s own free will is sufficient for fulfilling the Lord’s commandments, even when unassisted by God’s grace and the gift of the Holy Spirit for the performance of good works, the idea is altogether worthy of anathema and of perfect detestation.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: On comparing these words with the conclusion of this treatise before us, nothing will appear more probable than that the work which supplies the refutation of Cœlestius’ questions, which were also brought over from Sicily, was written not long after the above-mentioned epistle.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This work Possidius, in his index, places immediately after the treatise On Nature and Grace, and before the book On the Proceedings of Pelagius.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Augustin, however, does not mention this work in his epistle (169) which he addressed to Evodius about the end of the year 415; but he intimates in it that he had published an answer to the Commonitorium of Orosius, wherein that author stated that “the bishops Eutropius and Paulus had already given information to Augustin about certain formidable heresies.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Some suppose that this statement refers to the letter which they despatched to Augustin along with Cœlestius’ propositions.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: However that be, it is not unreasonable to believe that they, not long after Orosius’ arrival in Africa (that is, before the midsummer of the year 415), had sent these propositions to him, and that Augustin soon afterwards wrote back to Eutropius and Paulus a refutation of them, his answer to Orosius having been previously given.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Furthermore, Cœlestius, whose name is inscribed in the propositions, “wrote to his parents from his monastery,” as Gennadius informs us in his work on Church writers (De Scriptoribus Ecclesiasticis), “before he fell in with the teaching of Pelagius, three letters in the shape of short treatises, necessary for all seekers after God.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Afterwards he openly professed the Pelagian heresy, and published a short treatise, in which, besides other topics, he acknowledged in the Church of Carthage that even infants had redemption by being baptized into Christ,—an episcopal decision on the question having been obtained in that city about the commencement of the year 412, as we learn from an epistle to Pope Innocent (amongst the Epistles of Augustin [175, n.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 1 and 6]), as well as from the epistle [157, n.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 22] which we have referred to above; and from Augustin’s work On the Merits of Sins, i.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: 59; also from his treatise On Original Sin, 21; and his work Against Julianus, iii.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Another work by an anonymous writer, but which was commonly attributed to Cœlestius, divided into chapters, is mentioned in the treatise which follows the present one, On the Proceedings of Pelagius; see chapters 29, 30, and 62.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: There were extant, moreover, in the year 417, several small books or tracts of Cœlestius, which Augustin, in his work On the Grace of Christ, 31, 32, and 36, says were produced by Cœlestius himself in some ecclesiastical proceedings at Rome under Zosimus.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Augustin, at the commencement of the present work On the Perfection of Man’s Righteousness, mentions an undoubted work of Cœlestius as having been seen by him, from which he discovered that the definitions or propositions therein examined by Augustin were not unsuited to the tone and temper of Cœlestius.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This was very probably the book which Jerome quotes in his Epistle to Ctesiphon, written in the year 413 or 414.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: These are Jerome’s words: “One of his followers [that is, Pelagius’], who was already in fact become the master and the leader of all that army, and ‘a vessel of wrath,’ in opposition to the apostle, runs on through thickets, not of syllogisms, as his admirers are apt to boast, but of solecisms, and philosophizes and disputes to the following effect: ‘If I do nothing without God’s help, and if everything which I shall achieve is owing to His operations solely, then it follows that it is not I who work, but only God’s work is to be crowned in me.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: In vain, therefore, has He conferred on me the power of will, if I am unable to exercise it fully without His incessant help.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: That volition, indeed, is destroyed which requires the assistance of another.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: But it is free will which God has given to me; and free it can only remain, if I do whatever I wish.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The state of the case then is this: I either use once for all the power which has been bestowed on me, so that free will is preserved; or else, if I require the assistance of another, liberty of decision in me is destroyed.’”
